name: Sierra Nobla – circular from Puente de la Reina de Jaca via the Yesa reservoir
name_original: Sierra Nobla – circular desde Puente de la Reina de Jaca por el embalse de Yesa
description: This difficult 50.3-mile road cycling route from Puente de la Reina de Jaca offers stunning views of the Yesa reservoir and Pyrenean foothil
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Puente La Reina De Jaca traverse a landscape characterized by the Aragón River valley, the foothills of the Pyrenees, and the scenic Yesa Reservoir. The region offers varied terrain, from relatively flat sections along the river to significant ascents in the northern municipality, providing diverse challenges for cyclists. These routes often pass through rural scenery and charming villages, with some extending towards features like Sierra Nobla. The area's strategic location provides access to both well-paved roads and more demanding climbs. Best road cycling routes around Puente La Reina De Jaca The most popular road cycling route is Valle de Aragús y Jasa - Refugio de Lizara desde Puente de la Reina de Jaca, a 43.8 miles (70.5 km) trail that takes 3 hours 34 minutes to complete, featuring over 1,000 meters of elevation gain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Sierra Nobla – circular from Puente de la Reina de Jaca via the Yesa reservoir, a difficult 50.3 miles (80.9 km) path that offers views of the Yesa Reservoir and the Sierra Nobla range. Local road cyclists also love the Jasa – Refugio de la Loma de Aísa loop from Canal de Berdún, a 74.7 miles (120.3 km) trail leading through the Pyrenean foothills, often completed in about 5 hours 59 minutes. Road cycling around Puente La Reina De Jaca is defined by the Aragón River valley, Pyrenean foothills, and the Yesa Reservoir. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging ascents. The routes in Puente La Reina De Jaca are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 2 reviews. More than 460 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Puente La Reina De Jaca's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of road cycling routes are available around Puente La Reina De Jaca?
A: The region offers a diverse range of road cycling routes, from relatively easy rides along the Aragón River valley to challenging ascents in the Pyrenean foothills. You'll find routes with mostly well-paved surfaces suitable for various fitness levels. In total, there are over 20 road cycling routes, including 3 easy, 7 moderate, and 13 difficult options.

Q: What kind of terrain and landscapes will I encounter on road cycling routes here?
A: Road cycling routes around Puente La Reina De Jaca traverse varied terrain, including the scenic Aragón River valley, the foothills of the Pyrenees, and the picturesque Yesa Reservoir. You can expect to pedal through charming villages and scenic countryside, with stunning backdrops of the Pyrenees mountains. Some routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e930680276/sierra-nobla-circular-from-puente-de-la-reina-de-jaca-via-the-yesa-reservoir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra Nobla – circular from Puente de la Reina de Jaca via the Yesa reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, offer views of both the reservoir and the Sierra Nobla range.

Q: Are there any particularly challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, experienced riders will find several challenging routes with significant elevation gains. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31854814" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jasa – Refugio de la Loma de Aísa loop from Canal de Berdún&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 120.3 km trail with over 1,900 meters of climbing. Another difficult option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e927618653/valle-de-aragus-y-jasa-refugio-de-lizara-desde-puente-de-la-reina-de-jaca" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valle de Aragús y Jasa - Refugio de Lizara desde Puente de la Reina de Jaca&lt;/a&gt;, covering 70.5 km with over 1,000 meters of elevation gain.

Q: What historical landmarks or attractions can I see near the cycling routes?
A: Puente La Reina De Jaca is rich in history and culture. The town itself is on the Aragonese Way of St. James, and you can see the historic bridge over the Aragón River. Nearby attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/811905" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Monastery of San Juan de la Peña&lt;/a&gt; and the ancient &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2215505" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torre del Moro&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes also pass through charming settlements like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4622267" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jasa&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5258524" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arrés&lt;/a&gt;, offering glimpses into rural life.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Puente La Reina De Jaca?
A: The region is generally appealing for road cycling due to its diverse terrain. While specific seasonal conditions vary, the area's location in the Pyrenean foothills suggests that spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant landscapes. Summers can be warm, especially in the valley, while winters might bring colder conditions and potential snow at higher elevations.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, several circular routes are available. A notable one is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e930680276/sierra-nobla-circular-from-puente-de-la-reina-de-jaca-via-the-yesa-reservoir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra Nobla – circular from Puente de la Reina de Jaca via the Yesa reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, an 80.9 km loop. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31854814" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jasa – Refugio de la Loma de Aísa loop from Canal de Berdún&lt;/a&gt;, a longer 120.3 km circular trail.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Puente La Reina De Jaca?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 2 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from the Aragón River valley to the Pyrenean foothills, and the immersive experience of rural life and natural beauty. The low population density and limited car traffic in some areas also contribute to a pleasant cycling experience.

Q: Are there any easy or family-friendly road cycling routes?
A: Yes, among the over 20 road cycling routes, there are 3 classified as easy, offering less demanding options for those seeking a more relaxed ride or for families. These routes typically feature more gentle gradients and well-paved surfaces, often found along the Aragón River valley.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in Puente La Reina De Jaca?
A: While specific parking areas for each route are not detailed, Puente La Reina De Jaca, as a town, generally offers parking facilities. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival or consult local tourist information for the most convenient parking options near your chosen starting point.

Q: Is public transport available to reach the cycling routes?
A: Puente La Reina De Jaca is a well-known point on the Camino de Santiago, suggesting it has some level of public transport connectivity, likely bus services. However, specific public transport options directly to route starting points may vary. It's recommended to check local bus schedules or regional transport websites for detailed information on reaching the area and specific trailheads.

Q: Are there places to eat or get refreshments along the road cycling routes?
A: Many road cycling routes in the region pass through charming villages and scenic countryside. These villages, including &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4622267" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jasa&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5258524" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arrés&lt;/a&gt;, often have local cafes, restaurants, or small shops where you can stop for refreshments and experience the rural life of Aragon.
tips: Bagüés is a small rural nucleus that belongs to the Cinco Villas region in the province of Zaragoza. It is located between cereal fields and forested slopes. On the outskirts of the town is the fantastic Romanesque church of Santos Julián and Basilisa, from the 11th century.

Bailo dominates the lands on the left bank of the Aragón River as it passes through the intra-Pyrenean depression and assumes the role of an agricultural center, with large extensions of cereals and enormous expectations of irrigation.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is a difficult road cycling route, requiring a very good fitness level. It features significant climbs and descents characteristic of the Pyrenean foothills.

faqs.question: What are the road conditions like on this route?
faqs.answer: The road conditions can be variable. While parts of the N240 bordering the Yesa reservoir have a good surface, other sections might have unexpected tight and blind corners, or even rougher patches with grooves. Be aware of potential industrial traffic, including large trucks, especially near the reservoir.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 80.9 km long and typically takes around 4 hours to complete. However, this duration can vary depending on your fitness level, speed, and how often you stop to enjoy the views.

faqs.question: What are the main scenic highlights along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers breathtaking views of the cobalt blue Yesa reservoir and spectacular scenery of the Pyrenean foothills, including lush green landscapes and forests. You'll also pass through charming rural villages.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or villages along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes by the Church of Saints Julián and Basilisa of Bagüés and the Historic Village of Bailo, offering glimpses into the local heritage.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with parts of the Aragonese Way, the Via Sancti Martini - Chemin de Saragosse, the Viejo Camino de Santiago por el Valle de Hecho - Calzada del Summo Pyreneo, Camino Pintanos, Camino Pasarrés, and Camino Herrería.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route in Puente de la Reina de Jaca?
faqs.answer: While specific parking details are not provided, Puente de la Reina de Jaca is the designated starting point. Look for public parking areas within the village suitable for cyclists.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for race bikes on roads. While dogs are generally allowed in public spaces, bringing a dog on a demanding road cycling route of this nature is not typically recommended for their safety or comfort.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Pyrenean foothills can experience varied weather. Spring and autumn generally offer more moderate temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summer can be hot, and winter may bring colder temperatures or snow at higher elevations, making it less suitable for road cycling.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this public road route in the Aragon region.

faqs.question: Are there any services like cafes or accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: As the route passes through rural villages and near the Yesa reservoir, you may find limited services. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, and plan any stops for food or accommodation in advance, especially in Puente de la Reina de Jaca or other larger villages.

waypoints.name: Church of Saints Julián and Basilisa of Bagüés
